paper_metadata.title: Carbon pricing in climate policy: seven reasons, complementary instruments, and political economy considerations
paper_metadata.authors: Andrea Baranzini; Jeroen C. J. M. van den Bergh; Stefano Carattini; Richard B. Howarth; Emilio Padilla; Jordi Roca
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: Global review, with specific examples from Switzerland, British Columbia, and other countries
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Narrative review of theoretical and empirical work on carbon pricing, including studies of carbon and energy taxes and their public acceptability.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Narrative review of carbon pricing that highlights carbon and energy taxes, examining their distributional impacts, lobbying, revenue recycling options, and how these design features and political-economy factors influence public and voter acceptability.

determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Progressive carbon tax design protecting low-income households
determinants.definition: Carbon or energy tax designs that shield low-income or energy-poor households from net burdens via rebates, targeted tax reductions, or lower VAT on basic goods.
determinants.mechanism: Reducing regressivity and addressing inequality concerns makes the tax appear fairer, increasing stated acceptability and support.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: General designs of national carbon and energy taxes as discussed in empirical acceptability studies (refs. 70–73).
determinants.outcome_variable: stated acceptability and support for alternative carbon/energy tax designs
determinants.evidence_quote: A general finding of the literature on acceptability of climate policy, and in particular of carbon taxes, is that people tend to have a strong preference for designs that protect low-income households.
determinants.page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Summarizes multiple survey and choice-experiment studies on carbon/energy tax design; no single numerical effect size reported.
determinants.notes: Underlying studies include Bristow et al. (2010), Brannlund & Persson (2012), Gevrek & Uyduranoglu (2015), Baranzini & Carattini (2017).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: Concern about income inequality [in Swiss 2015 energy tax ballot]
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Concern about income inequality (Swiss energy tax referendum)
determinants.definition: Voters’ concern that an energy tax replacing VAT would worsen income inequality compared with the status quo.
determinants.mechanism: Equity concerns led citizens to oppose an energy tax perceived as regressive relative to the existing VAT, despite its environmental rationale.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Proposal in Switzerland (2015) to replace the national VAT with a federal energy tax decided by referendum.
determinants.outcome_variable: vote choice in the 2015 Swiss referendum on replacing VAT with an energy tax
determinants.evidence_quote: Concern about income inequality is among the main reasons why 92% of voters (more than two million people) in a public ballot held in Switzerland in 2015 rejected a proposal for an energy tax to replace the national VAT.
determinants.page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Public ballot result: 92% of more than two million voters opposed the energy-tax-for-VAT swap.
determinants.notes: Summarizes findings from Carattini et al. (2016) on the same ballot.

determinants.definition: Energy tax structured so that higher-income households bear a larger net burden than low-income households.
determinants.mechanism: Progressive burden-sharing addresses inequality concerns and aligns the tax with prevailing norms of fairness, increasing stated acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Hypothetical progressive federal energy tax for Switzerland evaluated in a choice experiment alongside the 2015 referendum.
determinants.outcome_variable: stated acceptability of the proposed energy tax in the choice experiment
determinants.evidence_quote: The authors of this study show, using a choice experiment administered at the same time of the ballot, that acceptability is much higher when a progressive energy tax is proposed, provided that information on its distributional properties are made salient.
determinants.page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Based on a choice experiment at the time of the 2015 Swiss ballot; no quantitative marginal effects reported in this review.
determinants.notes: Underlying experiment reported in Carattini et al. (2016).

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: information on its distributional properties are made salient
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Salient information on progressive distributional impacts
determinants.definition: Explicit communication that the energy tax has progressive effects and protects low-income households.
determinants.mechanism: Making distributional impacts transparent corrects misperceptions about regressivity, reducing opposition rooted in inequality concerns and raising acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Information treatments in the Swiss 2015 energy-tax choice experiment that emphasized its progressive incidence.
determinants.outcome_variable: stated acceptability of the energy tax under different information treatments
determinants.evidence_quote: acceptability is much higher when a progressive energy tax is proposed, provided that information on its distributional properties are made salient.
determinants.page_or_section: PE Issue 1: Distributional Consequences of Carbon Pricing
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Choice-experiment evidence indicates higher support when progressive features are highlighted; exact effect sizes not reported in this review.
determinants.notes: Effect pertains to informational framing of the same underlying energy tax.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: earmarking carbon tax revenues ... for environmental projects or other issues
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Earmarking carbon tax revenues to environmental or climate projects
determinants.definition: Using carbon tax revenues to fund environmental projects, green innovation/diffusion subsidies, or climate-related funds (including adaptation in poorer countries).
determinants.mechanism: Aligning revenue use with environmental and climate goals increases perceived legitimacy and fairness of the tax, boosting social and political acceptability.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Generic national carbon taxes whose revenues are earmarked for green spending, innovation subsidies, or international climate/adaptation funds.
determinants.outcome_variable: public and political acceptability of carbon tax designs with different revenue uses
determinants.evidence_quote: Of course, earmarking carbon tax revenues, or revenues of auctioned emission permits, for environmental projects or other issues, can be done to increase the acceptability of carbon pricing.
determinants.page_or_section: More than Carbon Pricing; Two Ways of Implementing Carbon Pricing Globally – Carbon Pricing Through a Carbon Tax
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Authors note that survey-based acceptability studies find higher support when carbon tax revenues are devoted to environmental or climate-related uses.
determinants.notes: Includes uses such as innovation subsidies and climate adaptation funds, in addition to domestic environmental projects.

determinants.raw_name_in_paper: importance of environmental organizations in successfully spreading support for British Columbia’s carbon tax
determinants.standardized_candidate_label: Environmental NGOs’ lobbying and communication for carbon tax
determinants.definition: Active environmental organizations that advocate for and communicate the benefits of the carbon tax, helping to build and sustain support.
determinants.mechanism: Green NGOs’ advocacy and information campaigns counter industry opposition, shape public opinion, and thus increase political and societal support for the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: British Columbia’s revenue-neutral carbon tax introduced in 2008.
determinants.outcome_variable: support and political acceptance of the British Columbia carbon tax
determinants.evidence_quote: evidence from British Columbia’s carbon tax stresses the importance of environmental organizations in successfully spreading support for it.
determinants.page_or_section: PE Issue 2: Carbon Pricing and Lobbying
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Descriptive case-study evidence summarized from Murray & Rivers (2015); no quantitative effect size reported.
determinants.notes: Highlights interest-group support as a facilitator of carbon tax acceptability and durability.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Knowledge, information, and policy experience effects on acceptability of carbon pricing
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: The paper links imperfect information and ex post experience to resistance/acceptability of carbon pricing in general, but not specifically or exclusively to carbon or fuel taxes, so these determinants were excluded per tax-specific criterion.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.issue: Perceived co-benefits (health, air quality, energy security) and support for carbon pricing
exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Co-benefits are discussed as potentially increasing acceptability of carbon pricing broadly; the text does not isolate effects for carbon or fuel taxes distinct from ETS or other climate policies.
